items.title: Java Projects are hidden Gems
items.author: Sadiul Hakim
items.content:
It’s not always fair to judge a language just by looking at how many flashy it has on GitHub.

1. Different Ecosystem Cultures

  • came out in 2009 with a strong open-source-first culture, backed by Google. Most Go developers are used to sharing libraries and tools on GitHub. That’s why we see projects like Docker, Kubernetes, Terraform, Prometheus, etc.
  • , on the other hand, was born in the 1990s in a very . Most big Java projects ended up as closed-source commercial software (banking systems, ERPs, trading platforms, telecom billing, etc.). Many companies spent millions building them, so they never released them publicly.

2. Where Java Shines (but hidden from GitHub)

  • : Banks, insurance, telecom, governments, healthcare, airlines — tons of their “mission-critical” apps are Java. These don’t live on GitHub.
  • : Large enterprise software products (SAP modules, Atlassian Jira/Confluence, Eclipse IDE, Android SDK tools, etc.) are Java-based, but not always open source.
  • : A lot of middleware, financial trading platforms, logistics and ERP backends, etc. are in Java but owned by companies.
  • Open source projects get on GitHub.
  • Closed-source projects may have 10x more code, complexity, and users, but we don’t see them.
  • So Go more active in OSS, while Java has a ton of “invisible” production-grade software.
  • Go was designed for cloud-native infrastructure → naturally leads to open-source tools.
  • Java was designed for → naturally leads to proprietary systems.

👉 So, judging Java by its open-source GitHub activity would underestimate its real-world dominance. Java may not have as many trendy OSS projects, but it has an enormous footprint in critical closed-source systems that power banks, governments, telcos, and Fortune 500 companies.

items.title: Robust Context in Go Microservices
items.author: Leapcell
items.content:
In modern microservice architectures, a single user request often triggers a call chain spanning multiple services. Effectively controlling the lifecycle of this call chain, passing common data, and “gracefully” terminating it when appropriate are key to ensuring system robustness, responsiveness, and resource efficiency. Go’s package is the standard solution designed specifically to address this set of problems.

This article will systematically explain the core design principles of and provide a set of best practices applicable to microservice scenarios.

Why Microservices Need Context: The Root of the Problem

Imagine a typical e-commerce order scenario:

  1. The API gateway receives a user’s HTTP request to place an order.
  2. The gateway calls the Order Service to create the order.
  3. The Order Service needs to call the User Service to verify the user’s identity and balance.
  4. The Order Service also needs to call the Inventory Service to lock the product stock.
  5. Finally, the Order Service may call the Reward Service to add points to the user’s account.

During this process, several tricky issues arise:

  • : If the Inventory Service gets stuck due to a slow database query, we don’t want the entire order request to wait indefinitely. The whole request should have an overall timeout, for example, 5 seconds.
  • : If the user closes the browser midway, the API gateway receives a client disconnect signal. How should we notify all downstream services (Order, User, Inventory) that “the upstream is no longer waiting” so that they can immediately release resources (e.g., database connections, CPU, memory)?
  • Data Passing (Request-scoped Data): How can we safely and non-invasively pass data strongly tied to this request—like TraceID (for distributed tracing), user identity information, or canary release tags—to every service in the call chain?

is Go’s official solution. It acts as a “commander” throughout the request call chain for controlling and passing information.

Core Concepts of context.Context

At its core, Context is an interface that defines four methods:

  • : Returns the time when this Context will be canceled. If no deadline is set, will be .
  • : The heart of the system. Returns a channel. When this Context is canceled or times out, this channel is closed. All downstream Goroutines listening to this channel will immediately receive the signal.
  • : After the channel is closed, returns a non-nil error explaining why the Context was canceled. If it timed out, it returns ; if it was actively canceled, it returns .
  • : Used to retrieve key-value data attached to the Context.

The package provides several important functions to create and derive Contexts:

  • : Usually used in , initialization, and test code as the root of all Contexts. It is never canceled, has no values, and no deadline.
  • : Use this when you are unsure which Context to use or when a function will later be updated to accept a Context. Semantically, it signals a “to-do” to code readers.
  • context.WithCancel(parent): Creates a new, actively cancelable Context based on a parent Context. Returns the new and a function. Calling cancels this and all derived child Contexts.
  • context.WithTimeout(parent, duration): Creates a Context with a timeout based on the parent Context.
  • context.WithDeadline(parent, time): Creates a Context with a specific deadline based on the parent Context.
  • context.WithValue(parent, key, value): Creates a Context that carries a key-value pair based on the parent Context.

Core Design Idea: Context Tree

Contexts can be nested. Using , , , etc., forms a Context tree. Cancellation signals from a parent Context automatically propagate to all child Contexts. This allows any upstream node in the call chain to cancel a Context, and all downstream nodes will receive the notification.

Best Practices for Context in Microservices

Pass Context as the First Parameter, Named

This is an ironclad convention in the Go community. Placing as the first parameter clearly indicates that the function is controlled by the caller and can respond to cancellation signals.

Even if you are unsure which Context to use, you should use or instead of . Passing will directly cause downstream code to panic.

Use Only for Request-scoped Metadata

is meant for passing request-related metadata across API boundaries, not for optional parameters.

  • TraceID, SpanID: for distributed tracing
  • User authentication token or user ID
  • API version, canary release flags
  • Optional function parameters (this makes function signatures unclear; pass them explicitly instead)
  • Heavy objects like database handles or Logger instances, which should be part of dependency injection

To avoid key conflicts, the best practice is to use a custom, unexported type as the key.

Context is Immutable; Pass the Derived New Context

Functions like , , etc., return a new Context instance. When calling downstream functions, you should pass this new Context rather than the original.

Always Call the Cancel Function

, , and all return a cancel function. You must call when the operation completes or the function returns to release resources associated with the Context. Using is the safest approach.

Not calling may prevent child Context resources (like internal goroutines and timers) from being released while the parent Context is still alive, causing memory leaks.

Always Listen to in Long-running Operations

For potentially blocking or long-running operations (database queries, RPC calls, loops, etc.), use a statement to listen to both and your business channels.

Passing Context Across Service Boundaries

Context objects themselves cannot be serialized and transmitted over the network. Therefore, when passing Context between microservices, we need to:

  1. Extract necessary metadata from on the sender side (e.g., TraceID, Deadline).
  2. Package this metadata into RPC or HTTP headers.
  3. Parse this metadata from headers on the receiver side.
  4. Use this metadata to create a new Context with as the parent.

Mainstream RPC frameworks (like gRPC, rpcx) and gateways (like Istio) already support Context propagation, typically via OpenTelemetry or OpenTracing standards.

gRPC Example (Framework Handles It for You):

is an indispensable tool in Go microservice development. It is not an optional library but a core pattern for building robust and maintainable systems.

Keep the following rules in mind:

  • : Make it a standard part of your function signatures.
  • Handle cancellations gracefully: In long-running operations, listen to and respond promptly to upstream cancellation signals.
  • : Ensure resources are not leaked.
  • : Only pass truly request-related metadata, and use private types as keys.
  • : Take advantage of native Context support in frameworks like gRPC to simplify cross-service propagation.

Mastering gives you command over lifecycle control and information propagation in Go microservices, enabling you to build more efficient and resilient distributed systems.
